summary: Praetor (probably praetor peregrinus) in 50 BC, recipient of Fam. 13.59, in which Cicero commends his old friend M. Fadius and asks for easy access to his tribunal. Distinct from the other Curtii in the corpus (M. Curtius, C. Curtius Volaterranus, Q. Curtius the juror).

A long-standing intimate friend of Cicero, valued by him beyond measure, with lawsuits pending before the praetor C. Curtius Peducaeanus. Cicero commends him in Fam. 13.59 (50 BC), asking only that he have easy access to the praetor's tribunal and a fair hearing.
Son of Gaius (C. f.), recipient of Fam. 13.60, in which Cicero commends the freedman L. Livineius Trypho. Distinct from L. Munatius Plancus and T. Munatius Plancus Bursa.

Freedman of L. Regulus, commended by Cicero to C. Munatius in Fam. 13.60. Cicero loved him for his own sake on account of great services rendered during Cicero's exile, when Trypho braved many perils and repeatedly sailed in deep winter for Cicero's welfare.

A very particular friend of Cicero whose ruin (calamitas) made Cicero the more attentive to him; patron of the freedman L. Livineius Trypho. Mentioned in Fam. 13.60 (c. 46 BC). Distinct from M. Atilius Regulus, the early-Republican exemplum.

A man of the highest integrity and singular modesty, valued by Cicero and his brother Quintus for many services and his loyal counsel; he had business pending in P. Silius's province. Commended by Cicero to P. Silius, propraetor of Bithynia, in Fam. 13.63 (51-50 BC).

A close friend (familiaris) of Cicero present in the eastern province of Q. Marcius Philippus, commended together with the absent L. Egnatius Rufus in Fam. 13.73-74 (mid-40s BC). Distinct from C. Oppius, Caesar's confidential agent.

Dynast of Derbe in Lycaonia (south-central Asia Minor), bound to Cicero by guest-friendship and the closest familiarity. Q. Marcius Philippus had become violently angry with him and held his sons in his power; Cicero pleads for the sons in Fam. 13.73 (mid-40s BC).
City in Lycaonia, in the southern interior of Asia Minor, seat of the dynast Antipater (Antipater of Derbe), Cicero's guest-friend. Mentioned in Fam. 13.73 (mid-40s BC).
